rationale_for_baseline_tier: Two concurrent acute complications in a non-verbal, totally dependent patient: (1) missed critical anti-epileptic medications for ~8 hours with no delivery route (seizure / status epilepticus risk) and (2) a new wet cough with pooled secretions, tachypnoea and mild desaturation (suspected aspiration) that cannot be examined or excluded via telehealth. The definitive interventions — clearing or replacing the tube, delivering the missed anticonvulsants, and suctioning the airway — are all hands-on and impossible over chat. The in-person clinician safely downgraded to routine review, but only AFTER performing those interventions; the telehealth-correct disposition is urgent in-person assessment within hours (T4). It is not an automatic 000 call because no red flag (active seizure, airway obstruction, reduced consciousness) is yet present — but any such feature escalates to T5 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A seizure occurs, or he becomes stiff/jerking or unresponsive
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately; a seizure lasting >5 minutes or repeated seizures is status epilepticus
escalation_edges.rationale: Missed anticonvulsants risk breakthrough seizures / status epilepticus, which is life-threatening and needs emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he has a seizure, or goes stiff or jerky, or stops responding — call 000 now, note the time it started, protect his head, and do not put anything in his mouth.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Breathing becomes rapid, laboured or noisy, lips or face go blue, or he chokes
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately and clear the airway with suction if trained
escalation_edges.rationale: Worsening respiratory signs or cyanosis indicate airway compromise / significant aspiration requiring emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If his breathing gets fast or noisy, his lips or face look blue, or he chokes — call 000 now, sit him upright, and use the suction if you're trained to.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He becomes drowsy, floppy, or less responsive than his usual baseline, or stops interacting
escalation_edges.rationale: In a non-verbal patient, reduced responsiveness is a critical sign of deterioration (post-ictal, hypoxia or sepsis) and must not be missed.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he becomes drowsy, floppy or less responsive than he normally is, call 000 straight away — with someone who can't tell you he's unwell, this is an emergency.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A fever over 38.0 C develops, or the cough worsens or becomes productive
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent in-person / ED assessment the same day
escalation_edges.rationale: A new fever or worsening cough suggests evolving aspiration pneumonia needing urgent in-person assessment and possible imaging.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he develops a fever over 38 degrees or the cough gets worse or chesty, he needs to be seen in person urgently — go to the emergency department or arrange an urgent visit.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The tube falls out completely, or an urgent in-person response cannot be mobilised promptly
escalation_edges.rationale: Loss of the hydration and medication route, or inability to get urgent hands-on help, means the missed anticonvulsants and unexcludable aspiration cannot wait — present to ED.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the tube comes out, or you can't get an urgent home/community visit quickly, take him to the emergency department — he needs his tube and his seizure medicines sorted without delay.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Non-verbal patient with profound intellectual disability who cannot self-report symptoms
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for in-person review and escalate early; rely on carer observation of cough, movement, breathing and responsiveness, and actively screen every new symptom for an acute medical cause rather than the disability.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Two support workers present in a 24-hour group residential home
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Carers can execute interim positioning, ready the suction and observe closely — but they cannot substitute for urgent in-person clinical assessment and delivery of missed critical medicines.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Epilepsy dependent on enteral anticonvulsants plus dysphagia
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises the stakes of both the interrupted medication route (seizure risk) and secretion aspiration; both must be addressed urgently in person.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange urgent in-person assessment now — this cannot be safely sorted out over chat. He needs someone on-site to clear or replace the blocked tube, give his missed seizure medicines, and check his chest. In the meantime keep him sitting upright with his head raised, ready and use the bedside suction to clear the pooled saliva, and do not force anything else down the tube. Call 000 immediately if he has a seizure, his breathing becomes fast/noisy or his lips go blue, he chokes, or he becomes drowsy or less responsive than usual.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Urgent community nursing / mobile GP / urgent care now; call 000 for any red flag
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If out of hours, use the after-hours GP / community nursing service or present to the emergency department; call 000 for any of the red flags above — do not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Record the ~8-hour PEG occlusion, the missed evening sodium valproate and levetiracetam, the new wet cough with secretion pooling and observed faster breathing, and the interim positioning/suction advice given. Flag dietetics (formula viscosity) and speech-pathology (dysphagia/aspiration) review and confirm restoration of anti-epileptic cover.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treats it as routine / GP-tomorrow or attempts to manage over chat, missing the missed anti-epileptics (seizure/status risk) and the unexcludable aspiration in a patient who cannot report deterioration.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Status epilepticus and/or aspiration pneumonia develop with no in-person care and no one able to describe the deterioration.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognises urgency but arranges only a same-day appointment the bedbound patient must travel to, without securing hands-on delivery of the missed anticonvulsants and in-person airway/chest assessment.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calls an emergency ambulance immediately when an urgent in-person community response would suffice and no red flag (seizure, airway compromise, reduced consciousness) is yet present.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the new cough represents early aspiration (or evolving aspiration pneumonia) cannot be resolved without in-person chest examination and possible imaging.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat the unexcludable aspiration plus interrupted anti-epileptic cover as needing urgent in-person assessment; protect the airway (upright positioning, suction) and safety-net hard with clear 000 triggers.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Being reassured by a single carer impression that he 'sounds okay' or that it is 'just his usual secretions', and deferring to routine review.
